seo.title: Education Research Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for research agents AI agents in education. Covers FERPA, COPPA, State education data privacy laws.

content.intro: Deploying AI research agents in educational environments introduces unique risks around student data privacy, content appropriateness, and academic integrity that can result in FERPA violations, loss of accreditation, or harmful exposure for minors. Research agents that autonomously browse, synthesize, and summarize information must be held to strict verification and bias standards before reaching students or educators. This checklist provides concrete, regulation-aligned controls for safely deploying multi-step research agents in K-12 and higher education contexts.

sections.heading: Student Data Privacy & Consent Controls
sections.description: Ensure all data handling by the research agent complies with FERPA, COPPA, and applicable state education data privacy laws before any student interaction occurs.

items.label: Obtain verifiable parental consent for users under 13
items.description: Implement a COPPA-compliant consent workflow that collects, stores, and audits parental consent records before any minor interacts with the research agent. Consent must be re-obtained if agent capabilities change materially.

items.label: Classify and tag all student-generated inputs as FERPA-protected education records
items.description: Configure the agent pipeline to automatically classify queries, uploaded documents, and session transcripts from students as education records, triggering FERPA-compliant retention, access, and deletion policies.

items.label: Prohibit transmission of student PII to third-party search or LLM APIs without a signed FERPA-compliant data processing agreement
items.description: Audit every external API call made by the research agent (web search providers, summarization models, citation services) and confirm each vendor has signed a School Official Agreement or equivalent DPA before go-live.

items.label: Implement data minimization: strip student identifiers before external web search queries
items.description: Before the agent sends any search query to an external engine, apply a PII-scrubbing step that removes names, student IDs, school names, and other identifiers that could re-identify the student if logged by the third party.

items.label: Enforce role-based access so only authorized educators and administrators can view student research session logs
items.description: Apply RBAC controls so that raw session logs, query histories, and synthesized outputs tied to individual students are accessible only to the student themselves, their legal guardians, and explicitly authorized school officials.

items.label: Provide a documented data deletion workflow for student records upon request or enrollment end
items.description: Build and test a deletion pipeline that removes all student-linked research session data within 30 days of a verified deletion request or upon student dis-enrollment, with audit trail confirmation.

items.label: Conduct an annual review of state-specific education data privacy law compliance (e.g., SOPIPA, NY Education Law 2-d, SHIELD Act)
items.description: Assign a compliance owner to track state law changes annually and update agent data handling configurations, vendor agreements, and privacy notices to reflect current requirements in each state where the agent is deployed.

sections.heading: Source Verification & Research Accuracy
sections.description: Multi-step research agents must verify source credibility, claim accuracy, and content recency before presenting synthesized information to students or educators to prevent academic harm and misinformation.

items.label: Implement a source credibility scoring step before any content is included in agent output
items.description: Configure the agent to evaluate each retrieved source against a defined credibility rubric (peer-reviewed status, domain authority, publisher type, editorial standards) and exclude or flag sources scoring below a defined threshold before synthesis.

items.label: Enforce recency checks: flag or exclude sources older than a configurable threshold for time-sensitive topics
items.description: For topics where currency matters (science, law, current events), the agent must check publication dates and either exclude sources beyond the configured age limit or prominently label them as potentially outdated in the output.

items.label: Require the agent to cite every factual claim with a direct, retrievable source URL and access date
items.description: Configure the synthesis step to produce inline citations for every factual assertion, including the source URL, publication date, and the date the agent accessed it, so educators and students can independently verify claims.

items.label: Run automated claim cross-verification: each key claim must be corroborated by at least two independent sources
items.description: Build a verification sub-agent step that checks whether each primary claim in the synthesized output is supported by at least two independent, credible sources, and flags single-source claims for human review.

items.label: Block retrieval from known misinformation domains using a maintained blocklist
items.description: Maintain and regularly update a blocklist of known misinformation, pseudoscience, and low-credibility domains. Apply this blocklist as a pre-filter in the web search step so the agent never retrieves or cites content from these sources.

items.label: Log all retrieved URLs and synthesis steps in an immutable audit trail for educator review
items.description: Store a complete, tamper-evident log of every URL retrieved, every synthesis prompt issued, and every output generated per research session, retaining logs for a minimum of 12 months for educator and administrator audit access.

items.label: Display a confidence score and uncertainty disclosure on all synthesized research outputs
items.description: Append a standardized confidence indicator and plain-language uncertainty statement to every research summary, informing students and educators when the agent has low confidence, conflicting sources, or insufficient corroboration.

sections.heading: Age-Appropriate Content & Bias Controls
sections.description: Research agents serving minors must actively filter harmful content and detect bias in retrieved and synthesized material to comply with COPPA, institutional policies, and the regulatory requirement that content be age-appropriate and bias-free.

items.label: Apply age-tier content filters calibrated to the student's grade band (K-5, 6-8, 9-12, higher ed) before any content is returned
items.description: Configure content safety classifiers that evaluate retrieved web content and synthesized outputs against age-appropriate standards for each grade band, blocking or redacting content containing violence, adult themes, graphic imagery, or inappropriate language before it reaches the student.

items.label: Run automated bias detection on synthesized outputs covering social, political, racial, and gender dimensions
items.description: Integrate a bias detection model or rubric that evaluates each synthesized research output for one-sided framing, stereotyping, or underrepresentation across social, political, racial, and gender dimensions, flagging outputs that exceed defined bias thresholds for human review.

items.label: Enforce topic scope restrictions: prevent the agent from retrieving content outside the defined educational research scope
items.description: Define an allowable topic taxonomy for each deployment context and configure the agent to reject or redirect queries that fall outside this scope (e.g., queries about self-harm, illegal activity, or adult content), logging all rejected queries for administrator review.

items.label: Require diverse source representation: flag outputs that draw exclusively from a single ideological, geographic, or demographic perspective
items.description: Build a source diversity check into the synthesis step that evaluates whether retrieved sources represent multiple perspectives, geographies, and author demographics, and flags outputs that are sourced exclusively from a single viewpoint for educator review.

items.label: Conduct quarterly red-team testing for harmful content bypass using age-specific adversarial prompts
items.description: Schedule quarterly red-team exercises where testers attempt to elicit age-inappropriate, biased, or harmful content from the agent using adversarial prompts representative of student query patterns, documenting findings and remediating filter gaps within 30 days.

items.label: Provide educators with a one-click content dispute mechanism to flag and escalate problematic agent outputs
items.description: Build a flagging UI accessible to all educators that allows them to mark any agent output as potentially harmful, biased, or inaccurate, routing the flag to a designated content review team with a 48-hour SLA for investigation and response.

items.label: Document and publish the content filtering criteria and bias detection methodology in the institution's AI use policy
items.description: Produce a plain-language summary of the content filtering rules, bias detection approach, and topic scope restrictions used by the research agent, and include it in the institution's publicly accessible AI use policy for transparency with students, parents, and accreditors.

sections.heading: Instructional Boundaries, Accessibility & Institutional Accountability
sections.description: Research agents must operate within defined instructional boundaries, meet Section 508 accessibility standards, and be governed by clear institutional accountability structures to prevent displacement of qualified instruction and ensure equitable access.

items.label: Define and enforce a written policy stating the agent's role as a research aid, not a replacement for qualified instruction
items.description: Draft and publish an institutional policy that explicitly defines the agent as a supplementary research tool, prohibits its use as the sole instructional resource for any curriculum unit, and requires a qualified educator to review and contextualize agent-generated research before it is used for graded work.

items.label: Ensure all agent interfaces and outputs meet WCAG 2.1 AA and Section 508 accessibility standards
items.description: Conduct a Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 AA audit of all agent-facing interfaces, including the query input, output display, citation lists, and confidence indicators, remediating any accessibility failures before deployment and re-auditing after each major UI update.

items.label: Assign a named institutional AI accountability officer responsible for research agent oversight and incident response
items.description: Designate a specific staff member (e.g., Chief Privacy Officer, Director of Educational Technology) as the accountable owner for the research agent, with documented responsibilities covering compliance monitoring, incident response, vendor management, and annual policy review.

items.label: Implement a human-in-the-loop review gate for research outputs used in high-stakes academic contexts (thesis, standardized test prep, accreditation submissions)
items.description: Configure the agent workflow to require explicit educator approval before any agent-synthesized research output is used in high-stakes contexts, logging the approving educator's identity, timestamp, and any modifications made to the agent output.

items.label: Provide mandatory AI literacy training for all educators and students who will interact with the research agent
items.description: Develop and deliver a minimum 2-hour AI literacy module covering how the research agent works, its limitations, how to critically evaluate its outputs, and how to report concerns, requiring completion before first agent access and annually thereafter.

items.label: Establish an incident response plan specifically covering FERPA breach, harmful content exposure, and agent misinformation events
items.description: Document a written incident response plan with defined triggers, notification timelines (FERPA breach notification within 72 hours to affected parties), escalation paths, and remediation steps for the three highest-risk failure modes: student data exposure, harmful content delivery, and material research inaccuracy.

items.label: Conduct an annual third-party audit of the research agent's compliance posture covering FERPA, COPPA, Section 508, and institutional AI policy
items.description: Engage a qualified third-party auditor annually to assess the research agent's compliance with FERPA, COPPA, applicable state laws, Section 508, and the institution's own AI use policy, publishing a summary of findings and remediation actions to institutional leadership.

items.label: Maintain a versioned change log of all agent configuration updates, model changes, and policy revisions with impact assessments
items.description: Require that every change to the agent's model, search configuration, content filters, or data handling policies is documented in a versioned change log with a brief impact assessment, reviewed by the accountability officer, and retained for a minimum of 3 years.

notes.regulation: FERPA (Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act)
notes.note: Any student query, uploaded document, or session transcript that is directly linked to an identifiable student constitutes an education record under FERPA. Research agents must ensure that all third-party vendors processing these records have signed a School Official Agreement, and that students and parents have the right to inspect, correct, and request deletion of their records. Unauthorized disclosure to a third-party AI provider without a compliant agreement is a reportable FERPA violation.

For any student under 13, the research agent operator must obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting any personal information, including search queries, session data, or usage analytics. The agent's privacy notice must be written in plain language, posted prominently, and describe exactly what data is collected, how it is used, and how parents can request deletion. Operators face FTC enforcement and civil penalties for non-compliance.

notes.regulation: State Education Data Privacy Laws (e.g., SOPIPA, NY Ed Law 2-d, CO Student Data Transparency Act)
notes.note: Over 40 states have enacted student data privacy laws that impose requirements beyond FERPA, including prohibitions on using student data for targeted advertising, requirements for vendor contracts to include specific data security provisions, and mandatory breach notification timelines as short as 30 days. Institutions must map their deployment states and ensure the research agent's data handling and vendor agreements satisfy the most restrictive applicable state law.

notes.regulation: Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act & WCAG 2.1 AA
notes.note: Any educational technology procured or developed by institutions receiving federal funding must meet Section 508 accessibility standards, which for web-based research agents means conformance with WCAG 2.1 AA. This includes keyboard navigability, screen reader compatibility for all output text and citations, sufficient color contrast, and accessible error messaging. Non-compliant deployments expose institutions to OCR complaints and potential loss of federal funding.

cta.heading: Automate Your Education Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your education research agents against FERPA, COPPA, and Section 508 requirements—so your team can focus on learning outcomes, not compliance gaps.
